text: The surgeon confirms the tumor location at the ascending colon and begins the procedure by identifying and isolating the ileocolic vessels. The right mesocolon is mobilized using a medial to lateral approach. The ileocolic vessels are divided after clips are applied. The hepatic flexure and proximal transverse colon are fully mobilized, followed by the division of the coloepiploid ligament to accomplish transverse colon mobilization.

The surgeon approaches the left mesocolon from the medial side, identifying and isolating the inferior mesenteric artery and vein. The inferior mesenteric artery and middle colic vessels are divided following the application of clips.

The surgeon performs an endoscopic transection of the terminal ileum. A zero polypropylene suture is used to construct a purse-string closure of the rectal lumen. The rectal mucosa is marked circumferentially distal to the purse-string, and a rectotomy is performed approximately four centimeters above the dentate line.

The surgeon uses a continuous insufflation smoke evacuator device to maintain visibility during the procedure. The mesorectal dissection begins on the posterior and anterior planes, which are clearly identifiable, and progresses to the lateral sides where neurovascular bundles are visible. The mesorectal plane is mobilized in a circumferential pattern, continuing the dissection until reaching the Douglas pouch.

The surgeon creates the J pouch using two firings of a 100mm linear stapler. Real-time angiography with indocyanine green verifies the pouch's vascularization. The mesentery of the J pouch is dissected free from the duodenum to maximize reach. The pouch is then approximated to the pelvis, and an anastomosis is performed using a 31mm circular stapler after applying a purse string at the distal resection margin.
